items.q: Which has the longer wavelength: microwaves or radio waves?
items.correct: Radio waves — they sit at the longest-wavelength end of the EM spectrum.
items.misconception: Microwaves, because they are used in 'big' devices like radar.
items.random_wrong: They have the same wavelength because both are used for communication.
items.why: Students remember 'micro' as 'small' and assume the wavelength is the smallest, missing that 'micro' refers historically to the wavelength relative to radio, not to absolute size.

items.q: When a wave travels through water, what happens to the water itself?
items.correct: The water oscillates about a fixed point; the wave transfers energy, not matter.
items.misconception: The water moves along with the wave from one place to another.
items.random_wrong: The water turns into wave material and changes state.
items.why: Visually waves look like 'water moving across the surface', so students think water itself travels with the wave, missing that only energy is transferred.

items.q: What does a concave lens do to parallel rays of light?
items.correct: It diverges them — they spread out as if from a virtual focal point behind the lens.
items.misconception: It converges them to a focal point in front of the lens.
items.random_wrong: It reflects them back the way they came.
items.why: Students mix up convex (converging) and concave (diverging) lenses because the names are easy to confuse and the diagrams look similar.

items.q: Redshift of distant galaxies is evidence that:
items.why: It's the universe is expanding. “the universe is contracting” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The Sun is currently in which stage?
items.why: Correct answer: Main sequence. “Protostar” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If the universe is 13.8 billion years old, how many seconds is that? (1 year ≈ 3.15×10⁷ s; answer in 10¹⁷ s)
items.why: Exact answer: 4.35 x10^17 s. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
